## Missing Dispose call on local IDisposable Disposable 'HttpClient' is created but not disposed. ---

Use using var for each locally created HttpClient in the test methods so it is disposed automatically when the method scope exits.

Best minimal fix:

  • In Jellyfin.Plugin.JellyfinHelper.Tests/Api/ArrIntegrationControllerTests.cs, update each local httpClient creation in the shown tests:
    • var httpClient = new HttpClient(handlerMock.Object);
    • to
    • using var httpClient = new HttpClient(handlerMock.Object);
  • No functional test logic changes are needed.
  • No new methods or imports are required.

## Missing Dispose call on local IDisposable Disposable 'HttpClient' is created but not disposed. --- Use deterministic disposal for locally created HttpClient instances in the affected test methods.

Best fix: in Jellyfin.Plugin.JellyfinHelper.Tests/Api/ArrIntegrationControllerTests.cs, change local declarations from:

  • var httpClient = new HttpClient(handlerMock.Object);
    to:
  • using var httpClient = new HttpClient(handlerMock.Object);

This keeps the existing mocked factory behavior unchanged, while ensuring proper cleanup at method end (including after assertions). No new imports or helper methods are required.

## Missing Dispose call on local IDisposable Disposable 'HttpClient' is created but not disposed. --- General fix: ensure any locally created IDisposable is disposed deterministically, typically with using / using var so disposal occurs even if assertions fail.

Best fix here (without changing behavior): in Jellyfin.Plugin.JellyfinHelper.Tests/Api/ArrIntegrationControllerTests.cs, change the local HttpClient creation in the affected test to using var httpClient = new HttpClient(handlerMock.Object);. This keeps the same object passed to _httpClientFactoryMock, preserves test behavior, and guarantees disposal at method exit.

No new imports, helper methods, or dependency changes are required.

## Generic catch clause Generic catch clause. --- To fix a generic catch-clause finding safely, replace catch (Exception ex) with one or more specific exception types that reflect expected recoverable failures, and let non-actionable/system-level exceptions bubble up.

For this method, the best minimal-risk fix is to catch System.IO.IOException, which is a common concrete failure type during backup import (file/stream read/write/parsing pipeline issues) and still return the same 500 with logging message as before. This narrows the catch while preserving endpoint behavior for I/O-related import failures.
Change only in Jellyfin.Plugin.JellyfinHelper/Api/BackupController.cs, in ImportBackupAsync(): replace the generic catch (Exception ex) block with catch (IOException ex) and keep the block body unchanged. No new imports are needed because using System.IO; already exists.

## Generic catch clause Generic catch clause. --- Use **specific exception catches** for the request-body read block instead of Exception.
Best fix without changing functional intent: replace the generic catch with targeted catches that still return the same BadRequest payload and logging message.

In Jellyfin.Plugin.JellyfinHelper/Api/BackupController.cs, in the block around lines 112–125:

  • Replace catch (Exception ex) with:
    • catch (IOException ex)
    • catch (ObjectDisposedException ex)
    • catch (DecoderFallbackException ex)

Each catch should keep the existing log call and same BadRequest response, preserving behavior while satisfying the rule by avoiding a generic catch.

No new imports are required because IOException, ObjectDisposedException, and DecoderFallbackException are already available via existing using directives (System, System.IO, System.Text).

## Call to 'System.IO.Path.Combine' may silently drop its earlier arguments Call to 'System.IO.Path.Combine' may silently drop its earlier arguments. --- Use a combine pattern that cannot accidentally discard the temp base path: generate the child directory name first, ensure it is treated as a simple file-name segment, and then combine.

Best minimal fix in this file:

  • In Jellyfin.Plugin.JellyfinHelper.Tests/Api/ArrIntegrationControllerTests.cs, inside the constructor, replace line 27 with two steps:
  • Create tempDirectoryName from the existing literal + GUID.
  • Normalize with Path.GetFileName(tempDirectoryName) before passing to Path.Combine.

This preserves current behavior for valid names while guaranteeing the second argument is non-rooted and segment-like.

## Call to 'System.IO.Path.Combine' may silently drop its earlier arguments Call to 'System.IO.Path.Combine' may silently drop its earlier arguments. ---

Use a non-rootable path segment directly in Path.Combine, and remove the unnecessary Path.GetFileName(...) call.

  • tempDirectoryName is already generated as a safe single segment.
  • Removes the exact pattern CodeQL flags.
  • Keeps behavior identical (same resulting path) for current input.
  • No new methods/imports/dependencies required.

Best fix with no functional change: in the constructor, validate LatestResultFileName with Path.IsPathRooted(...) and throw an InvalidOperationException if it is rooted, then keep Path.Combine(...) as-is.

Change needed in:

  • Jellyfin.Plugin.JellyfinHelper/Services/Statistics/StatisticsCacheService.cs
  • Constructor body around line 36.

No new imports or dependencies are required (System and System.IO are already present).

## Inefficient use of ContainsKey Inefficient use of 'ContainsKey' and indexer.
Inefficient use of 'ContainsKey' and indexer. --- Use TryGetValue to combine existence check and retrieval in one operation.

Best fix in this file/region:

  • In UpdateBaseline_NewDirectory_AddedToBaseline, replace:
    • Assert.True(baseline.Directories.ContainsKey("/movies/Batman"));
    • Assert.Equal(... baseline.Directories["/movies/Batman"]...)
  • With:
    • Assert.True(baseline.Directories.TryGetValue("/movies/Batman", out var batmanEntry));
    • Assert against batmanEntry.Size and batmanEntry.Count.

This preserves test intent and behavior, removes redundant lookups, and satisfies the CodeQL rule. No new imports, methods, or dependencies are required.

## Missed opportunity to use Where This foreach loop implicitly filters its target sequence - consider filtering the sequence explicitly using '.Where(...)'. --- Use LINQ filtering explicitly instead of manually filtering inside foreach.

Best fix in this method: replace the loop that conditionally adds points with a single AddRange(existingPoints.Where(...)). This preserves behavior (keeps only points before currentBucketStart) while making filtering explicit and concise.

Edit only Jellyfin.Plugin.JellyfinHelper/Services/Timeline/GrowthTimelineService.cs in MergeSnapshotIntoTimeline, around lines 703–711. No new imports are required because using System.Linq; already exists.

💡 Result:

No, File.WriteAllTextAsync does not update an existing file atomically. Concurrent readers can observe a truncated or partially written file while the write is in progress. File.WriteAllTextAsync uses FileMode.Create, which truncates the existing file immediately upon opening. It opens the file with FileShare.Read, allowing concurrent readers. However, readers opening after truncation but before the write completes (or during streaming writes) may see a zero-length or partial file. This is confirmed by Microsoft documentation stating "If the target file already exists, it is truncated and overwritten," Stack Overflow analyses of source code showing FileShare.Read, and general file system behavior where truncation happens before content is fully written. The recommended pattern for atomically updating a JSON cache file is to write to a temporary file, then atomically replace the original using File.Replace (or File.Move with overwrite). This ensures readers always see either the old complete file or the new complete file, never a partial one. Example code: string tempPath = cachePath + ".tmp"; await File.WriteAllTextAsync(tempPath, jsonContent); File.Replace(tempPath, cachePath, null); // null skips backup File.Replace uses OS-level atomic operations (ReplaceFile on Windows, rename on Unix), making the switch instantaneous and safe even across processes. This pattern is evidenced in .NET source analysis, Aspire CLI disk cache PR using file locking + temp files, and articles confirming atomicity of File.Replace.
